title: The Ballad Of Mabel Clare
content:
آ آ آ آ Ye children of the Land of Gold,
آ آ آ آ I sing a song to you,
آ آ آ آ And if the jokes are somewhat old,
آ آ آ آ The main idea is new.
آ آ آ آ So be it sung, by hut and tent,
آ آ آ آ Where tall the native grows;
آ آ آ آ And understand, the song is meant
آ آ آ آ For singing through the nose.

آ آ آ آ There dwelt a hard old cockatoo
آ آ آ آ On western hills far out,
آ آ آ آ Where everything is green and blue,
آ آ آ آ Except, of course, in drought;
آ آ آ آ A crimson Anarchist was he,
آ آ آ آ Held other men in scorn,
آ آ آ آ Yet preached that evï؟½ry man was free,
آ آ آ آ And also ï؟½ekal born.ï؟½

آ آ آ آ He lived in his ancestral hut,
آ آ آ آ His missus wasnï؟½t there,
آ آ آ آ And there was no one with him but
آ آ آ آ His daughter, Mabel Clare.
آ آ آ آ Her eyes and hair were like the sun;
آ آ آ آ Her foot was like a mat;
آ آ آ آ Her cheeks a trifle overdone;
آ آ آ آ She was a democrat.

آ آ آ آ A manly independence, born
آ آ آ آ Among the trees, she had,
آ آ آ آ She treated womankind with scorn,
آ آ آ آ And often cursed her dad.
آ آ آ آ She hated swells and shining lights,
آ آ آ آ For she had seen a few,
آ آ آ آ And she believed in ï؟½womenï؟½s rightsï؟½
آ آ آ آ (She mostly gotï؟½em, too).

آ آ آ آ A stranger at the neighbï؟½ring run
آ آ آ آ Sojourned, the squatterï؟½s guest,
آ آ آ آ He was unknown to anyone,
آ آ آ آ But like a swell was dressï؟½d;
آ آ آ آ He had an eyeglass to his eye,
آ آ آ آ A collar to his ears,
آ آ آ آ His feet were made to tread the sky,
آ آ آ آ His mouth was formed for sneers.

آ آ آ آ He wore the latest toggery,
آ آ آ آ The loudest thing in ties,
آ آ آ آ ï؟½Twas generally reckoned he
آ آ آ آ Was something in disguise.
آ آ آ آ But who he was, or whence he came,
آ آ آ آ Was long unknown, except
آ آ آ آ Unto the squatter, who the name
آ آ آ آ And noble secret kept.

آ آ آ آ And strolling in the noontide heat,
آ آ آ آ Beneath the blinding glare,
آ آ آ آ This noble stranger chanced to meet
آ آ آ آ The radiant Mabel Clare.
آ آ آ آ She saw at once he was a swell,
آ آ آ آ According to her lights,
آ آ آ آ But, ah! ï؟½tis very sad to tell,
آ آ آ آ She met him oft of nights.

آ آ آ آ And, strolling through a moonlit gorge,
آ آ آ آ She chatted all the while
آ آ آ آ Of Ingersoll, and Henry George,
آ آ آ آ And Bradlaugh and Carlyle:
آ آ آ آ In short, he learned to love the girl,
آ آ آ آ And things went on like this,
آ آ آ آ Until he said he was an Earl,
آ آ آ آ And asked her to be his.

آ آ آ آ ï؟½Oh, say no more, Lord Kawlinee,
آ آ آ آ ï؟½Oh, say no more!ï؟½ she said;
آ آ آ آ ï؟½Oh, say no more, Lord Kawlinee,
آ آ آ آ ï؟½I wish that I was dead:
آ آ آ آ ï؟½My head is in a hawful whirl,
آ آ آ آ ï؟½The truth I dare not tell,
آ آ آ آ ï؟½I am a democratic girl,
آ آ آ آ ï؟½And cannot wed a swell!ï؟½

آ آ آ آ ï؟½Oh love!ï؟½ he cried, ï؟½but you forget
آ آ آ آ ï؟½That you are most unjust;
آ آ آ آ ï؟½ï؟½Twas not my fault that I was set
آ آ آ آ ï؟½Within the upper crust.
آ آ آ آ ï؟½Heed not the yarns the poets tell,
آ آ آ آ ï؟½Oh, darling, do not doubt
آ آ آ آ ï؟½A simple lord can love as well
آ آ آ آ ï؟½As any rouseabout!

آ آ آ آ ï؟½For you Iï؟½ll give my fortune up,
آ آ آ آ ï؟½Iï؟½d go to work for you!
آ آ آ آ ï؟½Iï؟½ll put the money in the cup
آ آ آ آ ï؟½And drop the title, too.
آ آ آ آ ï؟½Oh, fly with me! Oh, fly with me
آ آ آ آ ï؟½Across the mountains blue!
آ آ آ آ ï؟½Hoh, fly with me! Hoh, fly with me!, ï؟½
آ آ آ آ That very night she flew.

آ آ آ آ They took the train and journeyed down,
آ آ آ آ Across the range they sped,
آ آ آ آ Until they came to Sydney town,
آ آ آ آ Where shortly they were wed.
آ آ آ آ And still upon the western wild
آ آ آ آ Admiring teamsters tell
آ آ آ آ How Mabelï؟½s father cursed his child
آ آ آ آ For clearing with a swell.

آ آ آ آ ï؟½What ails my bird this bridal night,ï؟½
آ آ آ آ Exclaimed Lord Kawlinee;
آ آ آ آ ï؟½What ails my own this bridal night,
آ آ آ آ ï؟½O love, confide in me!ï؟½
آ آ آ آ ï؟½Oh now,ï؟½ she said, ï؟½that I am yaws
آ آ آ آ ï؟½Youï؟½ll let me weep, I must,
آ آ آ آ ï؟½I did desert the peopleï؟½s cause
آ آ آ آ ï؟½To join the upper crust.ï؟½

آ آ آ آ O proudly smiled his lordship then,
آ آ آ آ His chimney-pot he floorï؟½d,
آ آ آ آ ï؟½Look up, my love, and smile again,
آ آ آ آ ï؟½For I am not a lord!ï؟½
آ آ آ آ His eye-glass from his eye he tore,
آ آ آ آ The dickey from his breast,
آ آ آ آ And turned and stood his bride before
آ آ آ آ A rouseabout, confessï؟½d!

آ آ آ آ ï؟½Unknown Iï؟½ve loved you long,ï؟½ he said,
آ آ آ آ ï؟½And I have loved you true,
آ آ آ آ ï؟½A-shearing in your guvï؟½nerï؟½s shed
آ آ آ آ ï؟½I learned to worship you.
آ آ آ آ ï؟½I do not care for place or pelf,
آ آ آ آ ï؟½For now, my love, Iï؟½m sure
آ آ آ آ ï؟½That you will love me for myself
آ آ آ آ ï؟½And not because Iï؟½m poor.

آ آ آ آ ï؟½To prove your love I spent my cheque
آ آ آ آ ï؟½To buy this swell rig-out;
آ آ آ آ ï؟½So fling your arms about my neck
آ آ آ آ ï؟½For Iï؟½m a rouseabout!ï؟½
آ آ آ آ At first she gave a startled cry,
آ آ آ آ Then, safe from careï؟½s alarms,
آ آ آ آ She sighï؟½d a soul-subduing sigh
آ آ آ آ And sank into his arms.

آ آ آ آ He pawned the togs, and home he took
آ آ آ آ His bride in all her charms;
آ آ آ آ The proud old cockatoo received
آ آ آ آ The pair with open arms.
آ آ آ آ And long they lived, the faithful bride,
آ آ آ آ The noble rouseabout,
آ آ آ آ And if she wasnï؟½t satisfied
آ آ آ آ She never let it out.
meaning:
This evocative piece by Henry Lawson represents a masterful exploration of classic. The lines capture a profound emotional resonance, inviting the reader to reflect on the deeper themes of human experience and artistic expression.

In the broader context of Henry Lawson's bibliography, these specific lines stand out for their clarity and poignancy. Whether you are searching for inspiration or a moment of quiet contemplation, this work offers a timeless perspective that transcends its original era.

At Linespedia, we have curated this specific poem to ensure it reaches a modern audience. We recommend sharing these lines as a digital poster or using them as a thoughtful caption to connect with others who appreciate the enduring beauty of verse.
